chunk_summary: Non-response to nutrition therapy in children requires reassessment of adherence, screening for organic causes, and timely escalation to advanced workup or referral based on clinical severity and growth metrics.
learning_objectives: Understand timelines for expected weight change after nutrition therapy in children.
Identify key clinical signs warranting escalation in non-responsive cases.
Learn first-line diagnostic workup for faltering growth in resource-limited settings.
Recognize when to refer for specialized care in cases of severe malnutrition or safeguarding concerns.
key_facts: Improved meal frequency and energy density should produce measurable weight change within 2–4 weeks in uncomplicated cases.
Escalation is warranted if weight-for-length/height is < -3 SD or if symptoms like bilateral pedal edema, chronic diarrhea (>14 days), or developmental regression are present.
Screen for chronic infections (e.g., TB, HIV), celiac disease, inflammatory bowel disease, renal disease, congenital heart disease, and endocrine disorders.
First-line workup includes complete blood count, urinalysis, stool exam, and targeted tests like tissue transglutaminase IgA for suspected celiac disease.
Referral should not be delayed if the child is clinically unwell, dehydrated, hypoxic, has edema, or safeguarding concerns exist.
reference_quote: Improved meal frequency and energy density should produce measurable weight change within 2–4 weeks. (NICE NG75, 2017)
document_key_topics: Non-response to nutrition therapy in children
Timelines for weight change after dietary intervention
First-line diagnostic workup in resource-limited settings
